Chapter 13: The Mirror

The courtyard of the Süleymaniye Mosque held its breath.

Süleyman stood with the decree in his hand, the sun flaring against the gold thread of his cloak. Effendi remained kneeling, the marble cold beneath his shins. Ibrahim Pasha’s turban had toppled into the dust, his kaftan splayed like a pool of spilled ink.

Ceylan pressed Lala Şahin’s head down with a firm hand. “Stay low,” she whispered.

Lala’s breath came in ragged gasps, her gray hair tangled across the stones. “I should have cut my tongue out.”

Süleyman’s voice cut through the stillness. “You kept him.”

No accusation. No anger. Just the flat statement of a man who had expected a different truth.

Effendi’s fingers curled into the fabric of his robes. “I kept myself.”

Ibrahim Pasha’s head snapped up. His face had gone the color of old parchment. “This is an impostor! A thief of ink and lies!”

Süleyman ignored him. His gaze locked onto Effendi’s palm—pink scar tissue against the parchment of skin.

Ceylan watched from the corner of her eye. The birthmark on Effendi’s shoulder was hidden beneath his robe, but Süleyman didn’t need to see it. He knew. Had always known, in the shape of a crescent moon no man could forge.

Effendi met the Sultan’s eyes. “I did not come to claim a throne. I came to claim a name.”

Süleyman’s fingers tightened around the decree. “You let Ibrahim raise you from ash.”

A pause. The wind sighed through the mosque’s arcades.

“I let him think he had.”

Ibrahim made a choking sound. “You *let* me—” He stopped. Swallowed. Then his voice dropped, venomous. “You let me kneel for ink so you could make me kneel for blood.”

Effendi didn’t flinch. “Yes.”

Silence.

Then Süleyman spoke, low and measured. “Stand.”

Effendi rose, unfolding from the stones like a shadow lengthening at dusk. The robe pooled around his feet, revealing the crescent moon branded into his skin. The mark caught the light, pale and perfect.

Ibrahim’s breath hitched. His gaze darted between the birthmark and the decree clutched in Süleyman’s hand.

Ceylan felt her pulse in her throat. She had seen that mark before—once, in the baths of Topkapı, when Süleyman had bathed in silence after Hürrem’s death. She had thought it a trick of the steam.

Süleyman stepped closer. His shadow fell across Effendi’s scarred palm.

“You are my son,” he said.

Not a question. Not a confession. A verdict.

Effendi’s throat worked. “I am.”

Ibrahim Pasha let out a sharp laugh, high and broken. “Forgive me, Sultan. This man is a scribe. A nobody. He edits decrees in pencil!”

Süleyman’s eyes never left Effendi. “And yet he forged a decree in blood.”

Ibrahim’s face twisted. “A forgery! Any hand can—”

“My hand wrote the cipher in your ledgers,” Effendi said. “My hand stitched the binding on the imperial Qur’an. My hand forged the ink to dry in the shape of a crescent.”

Ibrahim’s fingers twitched toward the dagger at his belt. The guards tensed.

Süleyman raised a hand. Not a gesture of command. A signal.

One guard stepped forward. Another. They seized Ibrahim by the arms, dragging him to his knees.

“No!” Ibrahim thrashed. “You cannot believe this—this *wretch*—”

“You raised a boy to think himself dead,” Süleyman said. “You burned a child’s hand to erase a name. And all the while, you believed the prophecy would protect you.” He unfolded the decree, letting the parchment ripple in the breeze. “But the prophecy was always yours.”

Ibrahim froze. His breath came in shallow gasps.

Effendi watched, unmoved. “You feared the firstborn son would rise. So you ensured there would be no firstborn son. Only a scribe without a past.”

Ibrahim’s laugh was a broken thing. “And you let me do it. You let me tear your hand to make myself safe.”

Effendi’s voice was quiet. “I let you kneel.”

Süleyman exhaled through his nose. He folded the decree carefully, tucking it into his sash. Then he turned to Effendi.

“What do you want?”

The question hung between them, heavy as a blade.

Effendi met his father’s gaze. “I want justice.”

Ibrahim’s head snapped up. “You want *my* blood!”

Süleyman’s hand settled on Effendi’s shoulder. Not a blessing. Not a curse. A measure of weight.

Ceylan felt Lala’s fingers dig into her arm. The old nurse’s eyes were wide, wet with tears.

Süleyman’s voice was steady. “You kneel before me, Ibrahim Pasha. Before the Sultan. Before the blood of my house.”

Ibrahim’s body sagged. His turban-less head bowed low.

Ceylan watched Effendi. His hands were still. His face composed. But she knew him—the way his fingers tapped against his thigh when he lied. The way his voice went soft when he was afraid.

She stepped forward, breaking from Lala’s grip.

Effendi turned his head. Just a fraction.

Ceylan met his gaze. “You were never ash,” she said.

His breath hitched. Just for a second.

Then Süleyman spoke again.

“Effendi.”

The name hung in the air, new and strange.

Effendi turned back to the Sultan. “Yes.”

Süleyman’s voice dropped, private. “You have my ear, Prince.”

The courtyard fell silent.

A prince.

Not an effendi.

Not a scribe.

A prince.

Effendi stood in the sun, scarred and steady.

Then he opened his mouth to speak.

- MINIMUM 900 words, target 1000-1200
- Short paragraphs (2-3 sentences max)
- At least 40% dialogue
- End on a cliffhanger that is DIFFERENT from any previous chapter's ending
- Commercial, accessible prose — the prose should be invisible
- Do NOT reference events from future beats — the characters don't know what's coming
- Match the emotional temperature from the beat entry
- Ground every scene in a SPECIFIC location from the bible
- Characters should act, not just talk about acting
- If the love interest appears in this chapter's beat, give them meaningful interaction with the protagonist — tension, attraction, conflict, or vulnerability. Don't just mention them in passing.

Output ONLY the chapter text. No author notes, no commentary. Start with "Chapter 14: [Title]"

calls.response_content:
Chapter 14: The Terms

The Divan Hall smelled of rosewater and old grief. Süleyman sat on the ivory-inlaid bench, his boots polished to a mirror’s sheen. The decree lay unrolled on the low table between them—a serpent of parchment, ink still damp in the shape of a crescent moon.

Effendi stood with his hands clasped behind his back, scarred palm hidden. Ibrahim Pasha knelt at the Sultan’s feet, head bowed, turban gone, his kaftan crusted with the dust of the courtyard. The grand vizier’s fingers trembled against the marble.

Süleyman tilted his head. “You burned a child’s hand.”

Ibrahim’s chest hitched. “A necessary fiction, my Sultan. The prophecy—”

“The prophecy named *you*,” Süleyman said. “Not him. Not any prince. You.”

A muscle jumped in Ibrahim’s jaw. “Then the child should have died. Not my reputation.”

Effendi’s gaze flicked to the brazier in the corner, its coals long cold. The scent of scorched flesh still clung to the hall’s edges.

Süleyman exhaled through his nose. “I will not execute a man for caution.”

Ibrahim’s head snapped up. “Then what? Pity?”

“Justice.” Süleyman’s voice was quiet. “You will leave Istanbul before dawn. No retinue. No gold. A single slave to carry your prayer rug and one change of clothes. You will travel by foot to Mecca and remain there under guard until I decide your fate is no longer my concern.”

Ibrahim’s face twisted. “Banishment? After I have—”

“After you have burned a boy’s hand to fake a death,” Süleyman said. “After you have forged decrees and embezzled funds and believed your ledgers made you king.”

Ibrahim’s fingers curled into fists. “And Effendi? This *scribe*? He edits my words. He sits in my halls. He—”

“The name is Emir Karamanoglu,” Süleyman said. “And he is my son.”

Silence. The wind rattled the lattice screens. Dust motes swirled in the slanted light.

Ibrahim’s breath came fast. “Then let him face trial. Let the Divan weigh the evidence. Let the empire decide if a man who edits imperial decrees with a pencil is fit to rule.”

Effendi stepped forward. The hem of his robe whispered against the floor. “The empire need not decide anything.”

Ibrahim’s eyes burned into him. “Oh? You’ve already written the verdict in your little cipher books?”

“No,” Effendi said. “I gave you a choice.”

Süleyman leaned back. “You have my ear, Prince.”

Effendi met Süleyman’s gaze. “I want him tried in the scriptorium. Open to the guild. Let every scribe see what he did. Let them hold the ledgers. Let them read the bloodstains he tried to erase.”

Ibrahim let out a sharp laugh. “You want humiliation? Fine. Let them see the man who ruled through numbers reduced to a thief of ink.”

“No,” Effendi said. “I want them to see the man who burned a child’s hand.”

Süleyman’s fingers tapped once against the table. “And if he refuses?”

“Then he forfeits mercy,” Effendi said. “And faces the blade.”

Ibrahim’s face went slack. “You would kill a man in cold blood?”

“No,” Effendi said. “I would let the guild decide.”

Ibrahim’s eyes darted to the door. Two guards stood there, hands on hilts. No emotion in their faces.

Süleyman sighed. “Ibrahim. You have ruled my empire through ledgers and fear. Now you will be ruled by the same.”

Ibrahim’s shoulders slumped. He pressed his forehead to the marble. “Then I choose exile.”

Effendi nodded. “Pack your prayer rug.”

Ibrahim’s laugh was a dry thing. “I have nothing left to pack.”

Ceylan Hatun stepped from the shadows near the lattice. Her embroidered kaftan caught the light, gold thread glinting like trapped fireflies. She held a folded strip of linen in her hand, the guild’s seal stamped in wax still gleaming.

“I kept your secret,” she said to Ibrahim Pasha. “For years. Thought you were a patron of art. Thought you were just another scholar with too much gold and not enough mercy.” She unfolded the linen. Bloodstains bloomed across the fabric, old and dark. “But then I saw this.”

Ibrahim’s breath stopped.

Ceylan held out the strip. “Your signature. On the decree that sent him to the brazier.”

The hall held its breath.

Ibrahim’s voice was a rasp. “You were never a child to care about.”

Ceylan’s hand trembled. Not with fear. With rage. “No. But you were always a man who cared about ink. About proof. About names written in blood.” She let the linen drift to the floor between them. “You forfeited mercy the moment you burned his hand.”

Süleyman exhaled. Long. Slow. “Take him.”

The guards seized Ibrahim’s arms, dragging him to his feet. He didn’t struggle. Didn’t speak. Only stared at the bloodied linen as they dragged him toward the door.

Effendi watched him go. Not triumph. Not pity. Just the steady weight of a question answered.

Ceylan stepped closer, her perfume—cardamom and crushed vellum—replacing the scent of old grief. “You didn’t have to say it,” she murmured. “They would have known his guilt without your words.”

Effendi’s fingers brushed the hem of his sleeve. The scar on his palm pulsed faintly, as if it remembered fire.

“I know.”

Ceylan’s gaze flicked to his hand. Then to his face. “You’re trembling.”

“No.”

“You are.” Her voice dropped. “Not from fear. From something else.”

The guards’ footsteps faded down the hall. The door clicked shut.

Effendi turned to Süleyman. The Sultan’s expression was unreadable, but his eyes held the weight of a father’s silence.

“You have my ear, Prince,” Süleyman said again. “What would you have me do?”

Effendi met his father’s gaze. Steady. Unbroken.

He reached into the folds of his robe. Not for a dagger. Not for a decree.

For a quill.

The inkwell on the table glinted in the lamplight.

Effendi uncapped it. The nib caught the light like a sliver of moon.

Then he dipped.

calls.label: ch-15

messages.content:
You write serialized commercial fiction. Your prose is clean, fast, and invisible — the reader should forget they're reading. Every chapter ends with the reader needing to turn the page.

ABSOLUTE RULES:
- NEVER use these phrases: "eyes locked onto", "mind racing", "everything went black", "frozen in time", "echoing through", "couldn't shake the feeling", "little did they know", "sent a shiver/chill down"
- NEVER end a chapter with a mysterious phone call or a whispered word from the shadows
- NEVER have characters announce their emotions or intentions ("I'm going to take you down")
- SHOW don't TELL — reveal emotion through action, gesture, silence, and subtext
- Vary sentence length. Mix short punches with longer flowing sentences.
- Each chapter's cliffhanger must be a DIFFERENT type from the previous chapter
- You never break character or add meta-commentary

Write chapter 15 of a 15-chapter serialized novel.

STORY BIBLE:
IDENTITY
- Title: The Sultan’s False Hand
- Pitch: A disgraced scribe discovers he’s the lost heir to an empire—and the man who ruined him must kneel
- Setting: The imperial bookbinders’ guildhall in 1553 Istanbul, at the height of Süleyman the Magnificent’s reign

PROTAGONIST
- Name: Emir Karamanoglu, called “Effendi” in the guild
- Public face: A quiet, bookish master scribe who edits imperial decrees by day and scribbles verse by night
- Hidden truth: The secret second son of Sultan Süleyman, spirited away at birth to prevent a prophecy of fratricide
- The wound: A burn scar on his palm from the night his “protector” threw him into a brazier to fake his death

ANTAGONIST
- Name: Grand Vizier Damat Ibrahim Pasha
- Why they feel justified: He believes only a scholar-bureaucrat can save the empire from war-drained coffers and erratic rule
- What they're protecting or pursuing: Absolute control over imperial finances and succession, to safeguard his own family’s future dominance

ENABLER
- Name: Lala Şahin, former wet-nurse turned guild mistress
- Their relationship to the protagonist: She raised him in the scriptorium after his exile and still calls him “my little moon”
- Why they fail the protagonist: In a drunken confession she reveals the boy’s true name to Ibrahim’s spies, hoping to secure his safety—only to learn too late whom she named

CATALYST
- Name: Ceylan Hatun, chief illuminator
- Why they're dangerous: She recognizes an old poem’s cipher inside a newly uncovered royal archive—and realizes two pages are missing, proof of a hidden heir

WORLD
- Institution: The Royal Scriptorium & Imperial Chancery, a parallel bureaucracy of ink and gold that shadows the Divan
- Key locations: The Scriptorium’s marble scriptorium, the Vizier’s domed treasury office, the back-alley tavern called the Black Quill, the Süleymaniye Mosque scriptorium library
- What everyone assumes about the power structure: That the Grand Vizier, not the Sultan, truly rules the empire through ledgers and favors

STAKES
- What the protagonist secretly holds: A sealed decree of succession, signed in his mother’s blood, hidden inside the binding of the imperial Qur’an
- What's being taken from them: The right to be known as the eldest prince and heir, and the revenues and marriages attached to that title
- The tangible proof: A birthmark shaped like a crescent moon on his left shoulder blade, identical to Süleyman’s birthmark
- The ticking clock: The Sultan returns from the Persian campaign in seven weeks, and the Vizier has ordered the execution of all possible claimants before then

DRAMATIC IRONY
- What the reader knows from chapter one: That the scribe Effendi is actually the lost prince, and that every golden pen stroke he makes is calculated to rise within the guild
- What the antagonist won't learn until too late: That the missing illuminator, Ceylan, is the scribe’s secret lover and will deliver the final proof to the palace gate at the exact moment Süleyman’s procession passes

LOVE INTEREST
- Name: Ceylan Hatun, chief illuminator and former palace artist
- Their connection to the conflict: She once illuminated a love poem from Süleyman to his first wife Hürrem; it now holds the key to proving the prince’s birth
- What keeps them apart: She believes he abandoned her years ago for safer patronage, and he fears she’ll expose him before he’s ready
- What will bring them together: A night-time chase through the candlelit stacks of the Süleymaniye library, where she sees the birthmark while binding his arm in torn silk

CURRENT CHAPTER BEAT:
CHAPTER 15: The Throne
Summary: In the restored scriptorium’s marble hall, Emir Karamanoglu—now Prince Emir—edits a new imperial decree, his scarred hand steady, while Ceylan illuminates the margins with gold leaf, her tears dried, and Lala Şahin counts the days until the Sultan’s return on a ledger that no longer bears bloodstains.
Emotional temperature: triumph
Characters present: Emir Karamanoglu (Prince Emir), Ceylan Hatun, Lala Şahin, apprentices
Cliffhanger: The final image: Effendi’s quill pauses over the parchment, and the camera lingers on his left shoulder—where the crescent moon birthmark glows faintly in the candlelight.
Dramatic irony: The world now sees what it always should have: a prince in the ink, not the shadows.

NEXT CHAPTER BEAT (aim the cliffhanger toward this):
(Final chapter — no next beat. End with resolution, not a cliffhanger. The last image should rhyme with the first chapter's opening.)

PREVIOUS CHAPTER SUMMARIES:
Ch 1: Effendi’s hidden birthmark revealed to Ceylan, shifting power dynamics in the scriptorium.
Ch 2: Emir Effendi endured public humiliation from Damat Ibrahim Pasha over a minor decree error, vowing silent vengeance by documenting the insult in cipher.
Ch 3: Lala revealed Ceylan knows Effendi's name, forcing him to confront his past and the empire's reach.
Ch 4: Ceylan stole Süleyman’s cipher, met Effendi, and they fled through ventilation shafts as Ibrahim’s spies closed in.
Ch 5: Effendi was stripped of editing duties, Ahmet Bey—a non-scribe—appointed head calligrapher, and Ceylan’s theft of Süleyman’s cipher was revealed as Ibrahim’s men pursued her.
Ch 6: Effendi lures Ceylan to the Süleymaniye under false pretenses, revealing he knows about Süleyman’s cipher and her hidden birthmark, leading to a chase where they escape through a ventilation shaft with the boy’s help.
Ch 7: Effendi exposed the treasury's storage flaw, silencing critics, while Lala Şahin subtly warned him, leaving a folded alif hidden in his sleeve.
Ch 8: Ceylan learns the cipher wasn't stolen but hidden, Effendi reveals a ledger declaring Ibrahim Pasha's succession null, and a collector marks them as targets.
Ch 9: Lala Şahin confessed to Emir Effendi about revealing his identity, offering a signed confession to save him, but he revealed he had already infiltrated the treasury, leaving Lala realizing she was too late.
Ch 10: Effendi exposed Ibrahim Pasha's embezzlement, blackmailed Ahmet Bey into confessing, and revealed his forged imperial Qur’an and succession decree to Ceylan Hatun, who agreed to help him access the sealed archive.
Ch 11: Ibrahim discovers his betrayal, Effendi rescues Ceylan, and the false prince's plot is exposed.
Ch 12: Effendi revealed himself as Süleyman’s long-lost half-brother to force the Sultan’s recognition.
Ch 13: Süleyman acknowledged Effendi as his son, stripping Ibrahim Pasha of power and revealing Effendi's true identity.
Ch 14: Ibrahim Pasha was banished by Süleyman, his crimes exposed by Effendi (Emir Karamanoglu), with Ceylan revealing his bloodstained decree.

Ceylan held out the strip. “Your signature. On the decree that sent him to the brazier.”

The hall held its breath.

Ibrahim’s voice was a rasp. “You were never a child to care about.”

Ceylan’s hand trembled. Not with fear. With rage. “No. But you were always a man who cared about ink. About proof. About names written in blood.” She let the linen drift to the floor between them. “You forfeited mercy the moment you burned his hand.”

Süleyman exhaled. Long. Slow. “Take him.”

The guards seized Ibrahim’s arms, dragging him to his feet. He didn’t struggle. Didn’t speak. Only stared at the bloodied linen as they dragged him toward the door.

Effendi watched him go. Not triumph. Not pity. Just the steady weight of a question answered.

Ceylan stepped closer, her perfume—cardamom and crushed vellum—replacing the scent of old grief. “You didn’t have to say it,” she murmured. “They would have known his guilt without your words.”

Effendi’s fingers brushed the hem of his sleeve. The scar on his palm pulsed faintly, as if it remembered fire.

“I know.”

Ceylan’s gaze flicked to his hand. Then to his face. “You’re trembling.”

“No.”

“You are.” Her voice dropped. “Not from fear. From something else.”

The guards’ footsteps faded down the hall. The door clicked shut.

Effendi turned to Süleyman. The Sultan’s expression was unreadable, but his eyes held the weight of a father’s silence.

“You have my ear, Prince,” Süleyman said again. “What would you have me do?”

Effendi met his father’s gaze. Steady. Unbroken.

He reached into the folds of his robe. Not for a dagger. Not for a decree.

For a quill.

The inkwell on the table glinted in the lamplight.

Effendi uncapped it. The nib caught the light like a sliver of moon.

Then he dipped.

Write chapter 15. Requirements:
- MINIMUM 900 words, target 1000-1200
- Short paragraphs (2-3 sentences max)
- At least 40% dialogue
- End on a cliffhanger that is DIFFERENT from any previous chapter's ending
- Commercial, accessible prose — the prose should be invisible
- Do NOT reference events from future beats — the characters don't know what's coming
- Match the emotional temperature from the beat entry
- Ground every scene in a SPECIFIC location from the bible
- Characters should act, not just talk about acting
- If the love interest appears in this chapter's beat, give them meaningful interaction with the protagonist — tension, attraction, conflict, or vulnerability. Don't just mention them in passing.

Output ONLY the chapter text. No author notes, no commentary. Start with "Chapter 15: [Title]"

calls.response_content:
Chapter 15: The Throne

The scriptorium’s marble hall smelled of beeswax and rosewater. Candlelight pooled on the long tables where apprentices bent over parchment, their quills scratching in unison like a flock of starlings settling into dusk. Emir Karamanoglu—Prince Emir, as the guild now murmured—sat at the head table, his scarred hand steady above a fresh imperial decree. The words *by order of His Imperial Majesty, Süleyman Khan* glistened in gold leaf, the ink still tacky to the touch.

Ceylan Hatun knelt beside him, a silk ribbon threaded through her fingers like a second spine. She held a brush of weasel hair, dipped in liquid gold, ready to illuminate the margins. Her eyes were dry. The tears of the previous night had been burned away by fury and cardamom tea.

“Turn the page,” Emir said.

Ceylan lifted the decree with both hands, her sleeves sliding back to reveal the crescent moon birthmark on her inner wrist—visible proof of the secret bloodline she shared with Süleyman. The mark had flared the night Emir revealed his own birthmark to her in the Süleymaniye stacks. Now, it pulsed faintly in the candlelight, a private constellation between them.

She turned the parchment.

Emir dipped his quill. The nib caught the light like a sliver of moon. He wrote:

*By the pen of Emir Karamanoglu, Prince of the Imperial Blood, heir to the House of Osman.*

Ceylan’s breath hitched. She set down her brush.

“Stop,” she whispered.

Emir didn’t look up. “Why?”

“That’s not how decrees begin.”

“It is now.”

“You’re not just editing. You’re writing history.”

“Yes.”

She leaned closer, her sleeve brushing his forearm. The birthmark on his shoulder blade—hidden beneath the linen of his kaftan—glowed faintly in the candlelight, a secret constellation mirrored on her wrist.

“You’re claiming the throne before the Sultan speaks.”

Emir dipped the quill again. “The throne doesn’t speak. Men do. And the men of this guild already know who wrote this.”

Ceylan’s fingers tightened around the silk ribbon. “They know the scribe. Not the prince.”

“Then let them learn both.”

She exhaled through her nose. “You’re reckless.”

“No. I’m editing.”

A silence settled between them, thick as candle smoke. Across the hall, Lala Şahin counted out silver coins on a ledger, her back turned, but her ears were not. The old nurse’s shoulders were rigid, her fingers trembling not with age but with the ghost of a confession she had never made.

Emir’s quill paused. He studied the decree, then the apprentices at the far tables. Ahmet Bey, the former head calligrapher, wiped his brow with a trembling hand. His guild pin—the silver crescent—had been ripped from his collar that morning. He was a man unmoored.

Ceylan followed Emir’s gaze. “He’s watching you.”

Emir didn’t turn. “I know.”

“He blames you for his fall.”

“He blames the ledgers. The proof. The truth.” Emir dipped the quill once more. “He will have to live with it.”

Ceylan’s voice dropped. “And Ibrahim Pasha?”

Emir’s hand stilled. “Gone.”

“But not forgotten.”

“Nothing is forgotten in ink.”

She reached for her brush again, but her fingers hovered over the parchment. “You’re not just claiming a title, Emir. You’re rewriting the empire in gold leaf.”

“Aren’t we all?”

She almost laughed. Instead, she set the brush down with deliberate care. “You should have waited for Süleyman to return.”

Emir finally looked at her. His eyes were dark, unreadable. “He returns in seven weeks. The Divan would have had him dead by then. The prophecy, the bloodstains, the embezzlement—all tied up in knots of fear. Ibrahim would have made sure of it.”

Ceylan’s throat tightened. “And now?”

“Now the ink is drying while the coals are still warm.” Emir capped the inkwell. “The guild sees what they see. A scribe editing a decree. A woman illuminating its margins. A nurse counting coins like she always has.”

Ceylan’s fingers brushed the parchment. Her birthmark—exposed, defiant—touched the edge of the decree. “And what do *you* see?”

Emir’s gaze flicked to her wrist. Then to her face. His voice was low. “I see the daughter of Hürrem’s chief illuminator. I see the woman who once painted Süleyman’s love poem in gold and lapislazuli. I see the only person who ever called me *little moon* when I was just a boy with a burned hand.”

Ceylan swallowed. “That boy is gone.”

“He’s here,” Emir said. “In the ink. In the gold. In the way the apprentices now bow to me before they bow to the Sultan’s seal.”

She exhaled sharply. “You’re not playing a game anymore, Emir. You’re playing for the throne.”

“No.” He uncapped the inkwell again. “I’m playing for the empire.”

Across the hall, Lala Şahin’s ledger snapped shut. The sound cracked like a whip. She stood, her shadow stretching long across the marble floor, and walked toward them with the slow precision of a woman who had spent a lifetime hiding her fear in ledger lines.

Emir didn’t look up.

Lala stopped beside Ceylan. “The apprentices are restless.”

Emir dipped his quill. “Good.”

“They whisper that you’ve claimed the title before the Sultan has acknowledged it.”

Emir wrote:

*By the pen of Emir Karamanoglu, Prince of the Imperial Blood, heir to the House of Osman.*

Ceylan’s fingers curled into fists. “They’re right to whisper.”

Emir capped the inkwell. “Then let them.”

Lala’s eyes flicked to Ceylan’s wrist, then to Emir’s shoulder where the linen of his kaftan gaped slightly. The crescent moon birthmark pulsed faintly in the candlelight.

The old nurse’s breath hitched. “You’ve been careless.”

Emir didn’t deny it.

Ceylan stepped between them. “It’s too late for carelessness.”

Lala’s gaze hardened. “No. It’s too late for *secrets*.”

Emir set the quill down. The tip trembled slightly against the table. “What do you want, Lala?”

“I want to know if you’ve won.”

Emir looked at the decree. The gold leaf caught the light, blinding and bright. “I don’t know.”

Ceylan’s voice was quiet. “We’ll know when Süleyman speaks.”

Lala’s lips thinned. “You think he’ll speak for you?”

Emir didn’t answer.

Lala stepped closer, her shadow swallowing Ceylan’s. “You’ve rewritten the decrees. Exposed the embezzlement. Blackmailed the treasury. Forged the succession. But Süleyman is still the Sultan. And he has not spoken.”

Emir’s hand rested on the decree. His scarred palm, pale against the gold, trembled once.

Lala’s voice dropped to a whisper. “What if he doesn’t claim you?”

Silence.

Ceylan’s birthmark burned against her wrist. Emir’s birthmark burned against his shoulder.

The apprentices’ quills scratched like mice in the walls.

Then Emir picked up the quill again.

He wrote:

*By the pen of Emir Karamanoglu, Prince of the Imperial Blood, heir to the House of Osman.*

The candlelight flickered.

The decree glowed.

Lala Şahin’s breath stopped.

And the camera lingered on Emir’s left shoulder—where the crescent moon birthmark pulsed faintly in the candlelight.

calls.response_content: Emir claimed the imperial throne by editing a decree, revealing his birthmark and bloodline, while Ceylan and Lala Şahin confronted the risks of his reckless power play.
